## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Gauntlet exit. A failure here is a real migration bug (a missed site), not
  exploration — triage is opus-grade.
  
  ## Goal
  
  The whole sprue migration AND the theurge-colophon consolidation proven
  end-to-end, and the discoverability promise verified: one grep per family
  returns the complete cluster — no bare enum values and no stale rbtd-
  colophons survive.
  
  ## Recipe
  
  - Run the gauntlet (`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh`) — pre-flight `rbw-MZ`,
    all crucibles quenched, per burn-bridges posture.
  - Grep audit:
    - `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/` / `rbnne_` / `bunne_` each return a complete,
      coherent cluster.
    - `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
    - `grep -rn 'rbtd-' Tools/ tt/` returns zero live colophons. Expected
      non-colophon survivor: the `rbtd-test-` scratch-dir prefix in the
      theurge Rust crate (rbtdte_engine.rs / rbtdti_invocation.rs) — code
      identifier, not a colophon. .claude/jjm history aside.
    - Spot bare-value audit: surviving `conjure`/`graft`/`tether`/`airgap`/
      `disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/`allowlist` hits are all class-1/3/4
      (prose, identifiers, coupled derived strings), zero class-2.
  
  ## Acceptance
  
  - Gauntlet green (via the new rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh name).
  - Grep audit clean per above.

  exploration — triage is opus-grade.

  The whole sprue migration AND the theurge-colophon consolidation proven
  end-to-end, and the discoverability promise verified: one grep per family
  returns the complete cluster — no bare enum values and no stale rbtd-
  colophons survive.

    all crucibles quenched, per burn-bridges posture.

      coherent cluster.

      non-colophon survivor: the `rbtd-test-` scratch-dir prefix in the
      theurge Rust crate (rbtdte_engine.rs / rbtdti_invocation.rs) — code
      identifier, not a colophon. .claude/jjm history aside.

      `disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/`allowlist` hits are all class-1/3/4
      (prose, identifiers, coupled derived strings), zero class-2.

  - Grep audit clean per above.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BM
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
6	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
7	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
8	`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
9	across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
10	fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
11	enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
12	work, prone to silent drift.
13	
14	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
15	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
16	uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
17	the sweep.
18	
19	The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
20	messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
21	durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
22	discovery recipe, not here.
23	
24	## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay
25	
26	"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
27	literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
28	character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
29	API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
30	textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
31	`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
32	build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.
33	
34	MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
35	an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
36	the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
37	prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
38	(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
39	inventing rationale.
40	
41	Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
42	dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
43	local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
44	(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
45	one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
46	text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
47	concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.
48	
49	## Convention — locked
50	
51	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
52	
53	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
54	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
55	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
56	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
57	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
58	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
59	             `e` = environment, etc.)
60	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
61	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
62	
63	Full target table (all in-scope enums):
64	
65	| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
66	|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
67	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
68	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
69	| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
70	| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
71	| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
72	| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |
73	
74	Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
75	egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
76	share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
77	DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
78	variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
79	whole cluster.
80	
81	## Scope decisions — resolved
82	
83	- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
84	- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
85	  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
86	  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
87	- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
88	  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
89	  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
90	- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
91	  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
92	  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
93	  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
94	  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
95	  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
96	  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
97	  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
98	- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
99	  unchanged from original framing).
100	
101	## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work
102	
103	Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
104	the candidate list and classify each hit:
105	
106	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
107	   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
108	2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
109	   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
110	   **Migrate.**
111	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
112	   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
113	   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
114	   alone.**
115	4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
116	   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
117	   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
118	   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
119	   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
120	     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
121	   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
122	   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
123	   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
124	   `-tether`.
125	
126	### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses
127	
128	- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
129	  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
130	  `:-bind`, etc.
131	- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
132	  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
133	  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
134	  stale.
135	- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
136	  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
137	  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
138	  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
139	  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).
140	
141	### Noise ranking (classification effort)
142	
143	Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
144	prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
145	`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
146	class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
147	is the heavy classification grind.
148	
149	## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate
150	
151	Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
152	(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
153	context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
154	literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
155	declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
156	remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
157	until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.
158	
159	**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
160	sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
161	graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
162	suites.
163	
164	## Discovery recipe
165	
166	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
167	  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
168	  enum missed.
169	- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
170	  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
171	- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
172	  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
173	  tables.
174	- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
175	  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
176	  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.
177	
178	## Posture
179	
180	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
181	execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
182	pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.
183	
184	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
185	`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
186	gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
187	remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
188	above.
189	
190	## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)
191	
192	₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
193	(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
194	not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
195	concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
196	wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
197	is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
198	intact.
199	
200	What remains and matters:
201	- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
202	  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
203	  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
204	  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
205	- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
206	  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
207	- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
208	  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
209	  any late bugfix churn there.
210	
211	## What done looks like
212	
213	- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
214	  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
215	- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
216	  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
217	  profiles) updated in place.
218	- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
219	  their selecting dispatch migrated.
220	- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
221	- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
222	- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
223	  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BM

## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
work, prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
the sweep.

The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
discovery recipe, not here.

## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay

"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.

MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
inventing rationale.

Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Full target table (all in-scope enums):

| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |

Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
whole cluster.

## Scope decisions — resolved

- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
  unchanged from original framing).

## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
the candidate list and classify each hit:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
   **Migrate.**
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
   alone.**
4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
   `-tether`.

### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses

- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
  `:-bind`, etc.
- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
  stale.
- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).

### Noise ranking (classification effort)

Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
is the heavy classification grind.

## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate

Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.

**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
suites.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
  enum missed.
- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
  tables.
- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
above.

## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)

₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
intact.

What remains and matters:
- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
  any late bugfix churn there.

## What done looks like

- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
  profiles) updated in place.
- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
  their selecting dispatch migrated.
- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.

## Out of scope

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
  by decision above.
- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:

1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
   graft cloud build.
4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
   heavy classification.
5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.

Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

# jjezs_pace ₢BMAAF

## Character

Gauntlet exit. A failure here is a real migration bug (a missed site), not
exploration — triage is opus-grade.

## Goal

The whole sprue migration AND the theurge-colophon consolidation proven
end-to-end, and the discoverability promise verified: one grep per family
returns the complete cluster — no bare enum values and no stale rbtd-
colophons survive.

## Recipe

- Run the gauntlet (`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh`) — pre-flight `rbw-MZ`,
  all crucibles quenched, per burn-bridges posture.
- Grep audit:
  - `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/` / `rbnne_` / `bunne_` each return a complete,
    coherent cluster.
  - `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
  - `grep -rn 'rbtd-' Tools/ tt/` returns zero live colophons. Expected
    non-colophon survivor: the `rbtd-test-` scratch-dir prefix in the
    theurge Rust crate (rbtdte_engine.rs / rbtdti_invocation.rs) — code
    identifier, not a colophon. .claude/jjm history aside.
  - Spot bare-value audit: surviving `conjure`/`graft`/`tether`/`airgap`/
    `disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/`allowlist` hits are all class-1/3/4
    (prose, identifiers, coupled derived strings), zero class-2.

## Acceptance

- Gauntlet green (via the new rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh name).
- Grep audit clean per above.

lastPrompt: mount BM

content.text:
Mounted **₣BM** (rbk-09-mvp-regime-sprues-misc), next pace **₢BMAAF** (final-regression-and-grep-audit).

## Pace goal

Prove the whole sprue migration **and** the theurge-colophon consolidation end-to-end, and verify the discoverability promise: one grep per family returns the complete cluster, with no bare enum values and no stale `rbtd-` colophons surviving.

## Heat context

- **Silks:** rbk-09-mvp-regime-sprues-misc — 10 of 11 paces done; this is the closing gauntlet-exit pace.
- **Shape:** Regime enum values (`conjure`/`graft`/`tether`/`airgap`, node modes, `BURN_PLATFORM`) were migrated from bare English words to sprue-prefixed values (`rbnve_*`, `rbnne_*`, `bunne_*`). Burn-bridges posture, no back-compat. Layered on top: the theurge `rbtd-*` → `rbw-t*` colophon consolidation landed in the most recent commit (₢BMAAK).
- **Recent work:** ₢BMAAK collapsed the theurge tabtarget family and retired the RBTW pipeline; verified on the cheap/local path (build clean, fast suite green, QualifyFast green). Heavy gauntlet/cloud regression was explicitly deferred *to this pace*.

## Docket

**Character:** Gauntlet exit. A failure here is a real migration bug (a missed site), not exploration — triage is opus-grade.

**Recipe:**
- Run the gauntlet (`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh`) — pre-flight `rbw-MZ`, all crucibles quenched, per burn-bridges posture.
- Grep audit:
  - `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/` / `rbnne_` / `bunne_` each return a complete, coherent cluster.
  - `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
  - `grep -rn 'rbtd-' Tools/ tt/` returns zero live colophons (expected non-colophon survivor: `rbtd-test-` scratch-dir prefix in the Rust crate).
  - Spot bare-value audit: surviving `conjure`/`graft`/`tether`/`airgap`/`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/`allowlist` hits are all class-1/3/4, zero class-2.

**Acceptance:** Gauntlet green; grep audit clean.

---

## Proposed approach

I'd split this into the cheap part (do now) and the heavy part (needs your pre-flight):

- **First, the grep audit** — read-only, fast, and it's the better failure detector for a *missed-site* bug. If a class-2 bare value survived, I find it here before burning a gauntlet run on it. I'll run all the family greps + the bare-value spot audit and classify hits.
- **Then the gauntlet** — `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh`. This is the destructive/expensive half: it does `rbw-MZ` (zeroes the regime to blank template), runs real cloud builds (conjure + graft — the vessel-mode acceptance gate the paddock flags), and assumes **all crucibles quenched**. I won't kick this off without your go-ahead.

One known wrinkle from the recent heat log: a **pre-existing ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID re-invest flap** (401 on freshly re-invested director SA, 2/3 runs) surfaced during dogfight verification — it's orthogonal to this pace, tracked under ₣BB. If the gauntlet trips on that, I'll triage as flap vs. real migration bug rather than treating it as a sprue failure.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential, opus — triage judgment is the whole point of this pace, and the gauntlet must not run in parallel with anything (shared regime + container namespaces).

---

Two questions before I proceed:

questions.question: How should I sequence this pace?

options.label: Grep audit first (Recommended)
options.description: Run the read-only grep audit now and report classified findings. Hold the gauntlet until audit is clean and you confirm pre-flight (crucibles quenched, MZ ready).

options.label: Grep audit only this turn
options.description: Do the audit and stop there; you'll drive the gauntlet yourself separately.

options.label: Everything now
options.description: Run the grep audit, then immediately kick off rbw-MZ + the gauntlet. Assumes crucibles are already quenched and you're OK with the regime being zeroed and real cloud builds running.

content.text:
`rbtd-` audit is clean: **zero live colophons** — no `tt/rbtd-*` files exist. The only survivors are (a) cargo build artifacts under `rbtd/target/debug/` (the crate is *named* `rbtd`; gitignored), and (b) the `rbtd-test-` scratch-dir prefix in `rbtdti_invocation.rs:29` / `rbtdte_engine.rs:44` — exactly the documented non-colophon survivor.

Now the bare-value class-2 detector. I'll hunt for the syntactic shapes a missed migration would leave (quoted equality, case arms, `:-default`), and also pull in the regime env files that live outside `Tools/`.

input.command:
cd /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster
echo "=== regime env file locations ===" 
find . -path ./Tools/rbk/rbtd/target -prune -o \( -name 'rbrv.env' -o -name 'rbrn.env' -o -name 'burn.env' \) -print 2>/dev/null | grep -v '/target/'
echo
echo "=== class-2 detector: quoted-equality / case-arm / default for bare enum values ==="
echo "(excluding target/ build dir, .claude/jjm history, .git)"
for v in conjure bind graft tether airgap disabled enabled global allowlist; do
  hits=$(grep -rnE "(= ?\"$v\"|=\"$v\"|:-$v\b|\"$v\"[,)]| $v\) |^\s*$v\)|== ?\"$v\"|'$v')" \
    --include='*.sh' --include='*.env' --include='*.py' --include='*.rs' --include='*.adoc' \
    Tools/ tt/ rbmm_moorings/ .rbk/ 2>/dev/null \
    | grep -vE 'rbnve_|rbnne_|bunne_|bubep_')
  if test -n "$hits"; then
    echo "--- BARE '$v' class-2 candidates ---"
    echo "$hits"
  fi
done
echo "=== detector done ==="

file.content:
# server still count as "reached" — the test is reachability vs
# connection failure, not authorization. Only honest curl failures (DNS,
# connection refused, timeout) trip the assertion.
# Args: variant (tether|airgap) path
zrbgp_write_posture_check() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_variant="${1:-}"
  local -r z_path="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_variant}" || buc_die "zrbgp_write_posture_check: variant required"
  test -n "${z_path}"    || buc_die "zrbgp_write_posture_check: path required"

  case "${z_variant}" in
    tether)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        "curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time ${ZRBGP_POSTURE_REQUEST_MAX_TIME_SEC} https://example.com || { echo \"tether: ERROR public unreachable (connection failure)\" >&2; exit 1; }" \
        'echo "tether: public reached"' \
        "curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time ${ZRBGP_POSTURE_REQUEST_MAX_TIME_SEC} https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo \"tether: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)\" >&2; exit 1; }" \
        'echo "tether: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write tether posture check"
      ;;
    airgap)
      printf '%s\n' \
        '#!/bin/bash' \
        'set -euo pipefail' \
        "if curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time ${ZRBGP_POSTURE_REQUEST_MAX_TIME_SEC} https://example.com; then echo \"airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)\" >&2; exit 1; fi" \
        'echo "airgap: public blocked"' \
        "curl -sS -o /dev/null --max-time ${ZRBGP_POSTURE_REQUEST_MAX_TIME_SEC} https://storage.googleapis.com || { echo \"airgap: ERROR google unreachable (connection failure)\" >&2; exit 1; }" \
        'echo "airgap: google reached"' \
        > "${z_path}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to write airgap posture check"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown pool variant: ${z_variant}"
      ;;
  esac
}

file.content:
local z_val z_stdout z_stderr

  buc_step "  Fact: systemctl is-enabled sshd = enabled"
  zbujb_admin_exec_native "${ZBUJB_CHIT_invigilate}sshd-is-enabled-" 'systemctl is-enabled sshd' \
    || true
  z_stdout="${ZBUJB_LAST_AP_STDOUT}"
  z_stderr="${ZBUJB_LAST_AP_STDERR}"
  z_val=$(<"${z_stdout}")
  z_val="${z_val//$'\n'/}"
  if grep -qiE 'no such|not found|not-found' "${z_stderr}"; then
    buc_die "sshd unit enablement: <unit not found> — operator handbook BUSJHL (apt install openssh-server)"
  fi
  test "${z_val}" = "enabled" \
    || buc_die "sshd unit enablement: expected enabled, got '${z_val:-<unreported>}' — caparison-linux (BUSJCL)"

  buc_step "  Fact: systemctl is-active sshd = active"
  zbujb_admin_exec_native "${ZBUJB_CHIT_invigilate}sshd-is-active-" 'systemctl is-active sshd' \
    || true
  z_val=$(<"${ZBUJB_LAST_AP_STDOUT}")
  z_val="${z_val//$'\n'/}"
  test "${z_val}" = "active" \
    || buc_die "sshd unit activeness: expected active, got '${z_val:-<unreported>}' — caparison-linux (BUSJCL)"

  buc_step "  Fact: sleep/suspend/hibernate/hybrid-sleep targets masked"
  zbujb_admin_exec_native "${ZBUJB_CHIT_invigilate}sleep-targets-" \
      'systemctl is-enabled sleep.target suspend.target hibernate.target hybrid-sleep.target' \
    || true
  local z_targets z_target z_i=1
  z_targets=$(<"${ZBUJB_LAST_AP_STDOUT}")
  for z_target in sleep.target suspend.target hibernate.target hybrid-sleep.target; do
    z_val=$(printf '%s\n' "${z_targets}" | sed -n "${z_i}p")
    test "${z_val}" = "masked" \
      || buc_die "${z_target} mask state: expected masked, got '${z_val:-<unreported>}' — caparison-linux (BUSJCL)"
    z_i=$((z_i + 1))
  done

  buc_step "  Fact: systemctl is-enabled tailscaled = enabled"
  zbujb_admin_exec_native "${ZBUJB_CHIT_invigilate}tailscaled-is-enabled-" 'systemctl is-enabled tailscaled' \
    || true
  z_stdout="${ZBUJB_LAST_AP_STDOUT}"
  z_stderr="${ZBUJB_LAST_AP_STDERR}"
  z_val=$(<"${z_stdout}")
  z_val="${z_val//$'\n'/}"
  if grep -qiE 'no such|not found|not-found' "${z_stderr}"; then
    buc_die "tailscaled unit enablement: <unit not found> — operator handbook BUSJHL (install + first auth)"
  fi
  test "${z_val}" = "enabled" \
    || buc_die "tailscaled unit enablement: expected enabled, got '${z_val:-<unreported>}' — caparison-linux (BUSJCL)"

  buc_step "  Fact: systemctl is-active tailscaled = active"
  zbujb_admin_exec_native "${ZBUJB_CHIT_invigilate}tailscaled-is-active-" 'systemctl is-active tailscaled' \
    || true
  z_val=$(<"${ZBUJB_LAST_AP_STDOUT}")
  z_val="${z_val//$'\n'/}"
  test "${z_val}" = "active" \

content.text:
Both confirmed **not class-2**:

- **`rbgp_Payor.sh:553/564`** — `zrbgp_write_posture_check()` switches on its own `z_variant` arg (`# Args: variant (tether|airgap)`), and the strings it emits (`"tether: public reached"`) are human-facing posture-check output. This is the paddock's class-4 witness — the worker-pool-variant derived token, not the `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE` comparison. Stays bare.
- **`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:2334/2368`** — compares `systemctl is-enabled` output (systemd unit state: enabled/active/masked). Different domain entirely, not `RBRN_ENTRY_MODE`. Stays bare.

Last check before declaring the audit clean — confirm the on-disk regime env files (which the gauntlet actually loads) carry the prefixed values, not bare:

# BUK Node Investitures (BURN)

Per-node configuration profiles describing the shape of remote targets. Project-global, git-tracked, shared across station users.

## Structure

```
.buk/rbmn_nodes/<investiture>/burn.env
```

Each subdirectory is a BURN investiture — one node identity. The `<investiture>` is the directory name; it is not redundantly inscribed inside the file.

## Fields

```
BURN_HOST=<ip-or-hostname>       # IP address or hostname of the remote node
BURN_PLATFORM=<platform>         # bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows
```

## Git Safety

BURN profiles carry no key material — they describe node shape only. Safe to commit; shared across station users.

## Validation

```bash
tt/buw-rnl.ListNodeRegime.sh                  # all investitures
tt/buw-rnv.ValidateNodeRegime.sh <investiture>  # single investiture
tt/buw-rnr.RenderNodeRegime.sh <investiture>    # show resolved fields
```

## Companion: BURP Privileged Investitures

Privileged credentials live at `.buk/rbmu_users/<user>/<investiture>/burp.env` — per-station-user, operator-authored. Each BURP investiture name IS a investiture name by construction; the `<investiture>` directory name must match a investiture directory under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/`, enforced by file-presence check at load time.

lines: BURN_HOST=<ip-or-hostname>       # IP address or hostname of the remote node

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Windows hive cleanup — reboot-as-canonical-primitive decision

Captured during pace ₢A-ABG (rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures /
windows-setup-first-time-debug) on 2026-05-11. This memo preserves the
design exploration that landed on **unconditional host reboot** as the
canonical Windows state-reset primitive for destructive cleanup, so
future maintainers can re-derive (or revise) the choice without
re-discovering.

## Symptom

On bujn-winpc, garrison-w's obliterate Phase 2 `Remove-CimInstance` on
`Win32_UserProfile` failed with:

> The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another
> process.
> HRESULT 0x80041017 (WBEM_E_INVALID_QUERY in the WQL probe;
> file-locked Win32 error in the destructive call).

Triage surfaced two stacked bugs:

1. **WQL backslash-escape bug.** The probe filter `LocalPath = 'C:\Users\<user>'`
   bombed at the WMI provider because WQL treats `\` as the literal-escape
   character in string literals; the path needs `\\` doubling. The
   bash-side `|| z_profiles_raw=""` fallback silently absorbed the
   provider error and downgraded "query failed" to "no rows," yielding
   *false-positive garrison-w success* while leaving six accumulating
   Win32_UserProfile rows (canonical + five `.rocket.NNN` demotion
   fallbacks).

2. **Hive-stuck race.** Even after fixing the WQL escape so the probe
   correctly returned the canonical row, `Remove-CimInstance` itself
   failed because the workload's NTUSER.DAT was still mounted under
   `HKU\<workload-SID>` (and `HKU\<workload-SID>_Classes`). The SSH-as-
   workload sessions garrison-w runs (`[w-session-1/4]` through
   `[w-session-4/4]`) cause UserProfileSvc to mount the hive on logon;
   on logoff, UserProfileSvc *eventually* unmounts but the race window
   between session end and unmount is large enough to hit destructive
   admin operations.

## Websearch — prior art

The phenomenon is well-known under the canonical observable **Event ID
1552** ("User hive is loaded by another process (Registry Lock)").
Microsoft historically shipped a separate tool **UPHClean** ("User
Profile Hive Cleanup Service") that monitored for logged-off users with
still-loaded hives and forcibly released holding handles; post-Vista,
UPHClean was integrated into UserProfileSvc with its own retry timer.
Industry-named contributing causes: antivirus scanners, Windows Fast
Startup, helper-process races (in our case WSL helpers `wslhost` /
`wslservice` / `vmwp` briefly holding handles to HKCU\Lxss subkeys).
Standard remedies in escalation order: wait/retry, manual
`reg unload HKU\<SID>`, `RUNDLL32 advapi32.dll,ProcessIdleTasks`,
reboot, safe-mode delete.

## Levels considered

A four-tier mitigation ladder mapped to escalating aggressiveness:

| Tier | Mechanism | Reliability | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `reg unload HKU\<SID>_Classes` then `HKU\<SID>` before Remove-CimInstance, gated on `Loaded=True` probe | ~80% (fails when non-WSL holders — Defender, Search indexer, vmwp — hold the hive) | ~40 LOC + WQL fix |
| 2 | Tier 1 + enumerate open handles via P/Invoke to `NtQuerySystemInformation`/`SystemHandleInformation`, kill non-essential PIDs holding workload-related handles | ~98% (UserProfileSvc itself remains possible holder) | ~150 LOC + empirical filter tuning |
| 3 | Tier 2 + `Stop-Service profsvc -Force; Start-Service profsvc` | ~99.5% (collateral risk on shared hosts) | ~30 LOC additional |
| 4 | Reboot (`Restart-Computer -Force`) | **100%** as long as the host boots | ~30 LOC |

A separate "Level 3 redesign" was explored: admin pre-creates the
workload profile via `CreateProfile` P/Invoke (userenv.dll), `wsl --import`
runs as admin, the vhdx is moved to a workload-owned path, admin deletes
its own HKCU\Lxss entry via raw registry (not `wsl --unregister`, which
would delete the vhdx), and the Lxss subtree is offline-injected into
workload's NTUSER.DAT via `reg load` / `reg unload`. **This was rejected
as a race fix** because the first real workload SSH use (the entire
point of having a workload account) re-creates the hive mount and
re-opens the race window. Shifting *when* the first hive mount occurs
(from setup to first use) doesn't eliminate the race. The
architecture's *independent* value (smaller code, no SSH-as-workload
during setup) is captured in the admin-no-WSL paddock discussion (see
pace ₢A-ABJ as reframed).

## Decision: Tier 4 (reboot) as the primitive

Empirically validated on bujn-winpc: pre-reboot showed workload SID
`S-1-5-21-…-1029` + `_Classes` mounted under HKU with `Loaded=True`;
post-reboot (uptime delta 15h → 48s, ~14s SSH return; second-cycle
~42s) all hives unmounted, all six demoted Win32_UserProfile rows
cleanly removable. Garrison-w converges end-to-end with reboot as
prelude.

**Rationale for picking Tier 4 over Tier 1/2/3:**

- **Determinism.** Tiers 1-3 are probabilistic; Tier 4 is binary
  (it works or the host won't boot).
- **No undocumented internals.** Tiers 2-3 depend on
  `NtQuerySystemInformation` handle enumeration, UserProfileSvc
  internals, and empirical tuning of process-kill exclusion lists.
  Tier 4 depends only on `Restart-Computer -Force` and SSH
  reachability — both first-class, stable interfaces.
- **Code surface.** Tier 4 is the smallest (~30 LOC for the helper)
  despite being the most reliable. Tiers 1-3 grew code without
  matching reliability gains.
- **Diagnostic surface.** A failed reboot is a single, well-
  understood failure mode (Group Policy block, pending operations,
  hardware refusing to boot). A failed Tier 2 handle-hunt requires
  bespoke debugging.
- **Cost.** ~30-60s per garrison invocation in our deployment (modern
  hardware, NVMe SSD). Garrison-w is a multi-minute operation; the
  reboot cost is rounding error.

**The reboot helper's load-bearing preconditions** (all delivered by
caparison-windows; cross-referenced in BUSJCW):

- sshd is a Windows service starting at boot.
- Tailscale service `StartType = Automatic` (BURN_HOST resolves on
  Tailnet after reboot without operator action).
- `administrators_authorized_keys` + sshd_config Match block persist
  across reboot (they live in `C:\ProgramData\ssh`).

## Note: Windows Fast Startup is not what we observed — and we bypass it explicitly anyway

It's tempting to attribute the ~14-42s reboot times to Windows Fast
Startup, but **Fast Startup applies to `shutdown` (hybrid shutdown
saving the kernel session), not `restart`**. `Restart-Computer` /
`shutdown /r` does a full cold boot per documented PowerShell semantics.
The fast wall-clock times we saw are modern UEFI fast boot + NVMe SSD
performance, not OS-level fast startup. The polling-cap rationale
(`BUJB_reboot_poll_cap_s='600'`, 10 minutes) is dominated by the
pending-Windows-update worst case, not by normal boot variance.

**Belt-and-suspenders explicit cold restart.** The helper dispatches
`shutdown.exe /full /r /f /t 0` rather than `Restart-Computer -Force`.
The `/full` flag is the documented hatch to bypass Fast Startup
behavior; while redundant on `/r` (restart) under current Windows
semantics, it makes the intent visible in code and is robust to any
future MS default changes. We are not relying on "restart happens to
do a cold boot by default" — we are asking for a cold boot explicitly.

## Implementation pointers

- Helper: `zbujb_reboot_and_await_ssh` in
  `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`.
- Wired unconditionally on `BURN_PLATFORM = bubep_windows` in
  `bujb_garrison()` between `bujp_preflight` and
  `zbujb_obliterate_workload`.
- Tinder constants: `BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_5`,
  `BUJB_reboot_poll_interval_s`, `BUJB_reboot_poll_cap_s`.
- Phase 2 obliterate simplified to WQL-escape + buc_die-on-probe-
  failure; no in-process unload remediation.
- Pace ₢A-ABG commits (`ff8d1e36`, `3fceebb3`, `086ab90d`,
  `08e26ec5`) carry the diff.

## When to revisit this decision

- If Windows ever ships a deterministic in-process hive-unload API,
  Tier 4 may stop being smallest.
- If we deploy to hosts where reboot is unacceptably invasive (shared
  workstations, long-running stateful Windows services) — currently
  out of scope per BUSJGW §Deferred.
- If pending Windows updates routinely extend reboot beyond 10
  minutes, we may need to raise `BUJB_reboot_poll_cap_s` or split the
  cap into "first-attempt cap" vs "update-install cap" with operator
  notification on the threshold cross.

file.content:
ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
    "canonical-invest"
    "dogfight"
  )
  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
  ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR=(
    "kludge-tadmor"
    "tadmor"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

content.text:
Here's the authoritative suite roster, straight from `rbte_engine.sh` (the resolver at lines 141–148) and the tabtargets in `tt/`. Eight suites, ordered cheap → expensive:

| Suite | Tabtarget | Fixtures | Needs | Cost / posture |
|-------|-----------|----------|-------|----------------|
| **fast** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` | enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke, handbook-render, dockerfile-hygiene | None | Pure local validation. (Note: now includes handbook-render + dockerfile-hygiene — CLAUDE.md's "75 cases" table is stale.) |
| **service** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.service.sh` | fast + access-probe, hallmark-lifecycle, batch-vouch | GCP credentials | No project churn; touches live GCP read/vouch. |
| **crucible** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.crucible.sh` | fast + tadmor, srjcl, pluml | Container runtime | Local containers; no GCP. |
| **complete** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh` | fast + service-extras + crucible-extras | GCP creds + runtime | Everything except the gauntlet ladder. |
| **tadmor** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.tadmor.sh` | kludge-tadmor, tadmor | Container runtime | Fully local — no GCP/depot/project. Builds both vessels, runs security cases. |
| **dogfight** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` | canonical-invest, dogfight | **Standing canonical depot** (hand-levied) | Cloud build → summon → run viability probe. **No crucible.** Spends cloud build/GAR, no new project. |
| **skirmish** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.sh` | enrollment-validation, canonical-invest, onboarding-sequence, regime-validation, regime-smoke, dockerfile-hygiene, tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml | **Standing canonical depot** + runtime | "Mini gauntlet" — depot→build→crucible chain *without* project-ID churn. Spends cloud build/GAR, creates no GCP project per run. |
| **gauntlet** | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh` | enrollment-validation, pristine-lifecycle, canonical-establish, onboarding-sequence, regime-validation, regime-smoke, dockerfile-hygiene, hallmark-lifecycle, tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml | Full release ladder | Levies fresh projects from marshal-zero. The destructive one — **out today.** |

Two things before I run anything:

1. **Your run list reads slightly contradictory** — "we won't run gauntlet today" but then "Skirmish, gauntlet, and all the other faster suites, yes." I'm reading that as **gauntlet OUT**, and skirmish + everything faster IN. Confirm if I've got that wrong.

2. **Skirmish (and dogfight) require a standing hand-levied canonical depot** as an operator precondition — `canonical-invest` reuses an already-`rbw-dL`'d depot rather than levying one. The known **ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID re-invest flap** (₣BB territory) lives exactly in skirmish/dogfight's `canonical-invest` lead. Is the canonical depot currently levied and ready?

Tell me the precondition state and I'll sequence the runs (sequentially — never parallel; fast → tadmor/crucible → skirmish), reading each `../logs-buk/` result and triaging failures as real-migration-bug vs. known-flap. Which suites do you want, and is the canonical depot up?

content.text:
Good — gauntlet's out (the fresh-project ladder levies/destroys two depots, and you're Cloud-Build-quota-blocked), depot precondition is up. So today's regression is the skirmish + faster-suite ladder, on both macos (here) and cerebro.

On structure — I agree with your instinct: the **macos run is already this pace** (₢BMAAF — its grep-audit half is done and clean), so we only need **one new pace for the cerebro leg**. That also mirrors the heat's two-platform posture (linux + macos) that the ₣BK start-gate established.

One wrinkle worth deciding cleanly: ₢BMAAF's docket currently makes "**gauntlet green**" the acceptance. If we're substituting the skirmish ladder because of quota, that acceptance line is now false-by-plan — I'd reslate ₢BMAAF so the record is honest (grep audit done + skirmish/faster suites as the available regression; full fresh-project gauntlet explicitly deferred). And the deferred true-gauntlet shouldn't just vanish — it needs a home so ₣BM doesn't silently claim coverage it didn't run.

Two decisions:

# jjezs_reslate ₢BMAAF

## Character

Regression exit, quota-constrained. A failure here is a real migration bug
(a missed site), not exploration — triage is opus-grade.

## Goal

The sprue migration AND theurge-colophon consolidation proven on this
station by the strongest regression available while Cloud Build is
quota-blocked — the skirmish ladder plus the faster suites — and the
discoverability promise verified by grep audit.

## Recipe

- Grep audit (DONE — clean): `rbnve_`/`rbnne_`/`bunne_` each a coherent
  cluster; `bubep_` repo-wide empty; zero live `rbtd-` colophons; zero
  class-2 bare-value survivors. Caught and repaired one harmonize miss —
  `BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows` in the `bujn-winpc` node profile (+ its
  README) — exactly the missed-site this pace exists to catch.
- Run sequentially on macos (never parallel; standing canonical depot is
  levied): the faster suites (`fast`, local `crucible`/`tadmor`), then
  `skirmish` (standing-depot mini-gauntlet via `rbw-ts.TestSuite.*`).
- Read each `../logs-buk/` result; triage any red as real-migration-bug vs.
  the known ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID re-invest flap (₣BB) seated in
  `canonical-invest`.

## Acceptance

- Grep audit clean (done).
- skirmish + faster suites green on macos.

Full fresh-project gauntlet deferred (Cloud Build quota); operator handles
out of band — not re-proven here.

The same skirmish + faster-suite ladder proven on macos re-run on the
cerebro Linux fundus — confirming the sprue migration and theurge-colophon

content.text:
`fast` suite is **green — 112/112** across all five fixtures (enrollment-validation 47, regime-validation 32, regime-smoke 9, handbook-render 15, dockerfile-hygiene 9). Critically, `regime-validation` exercises the migrated enum gates directly — `rbrv_conjure_baseline_valid`, `rbrn_invalid_entry_mode/dns_mode/access_mode` all pass against the `rbnve_*`/`rbnne_*` values. The sprue migration holds locally.

Before I spend the long container/cloud runs, a sequencing call — the local `crucible`/`tadmor` suites overlap heavily with what `skirmish` already charges (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml), so running them all is largely redundant container time:

questions.question: fast is green. How far up the local ladder before skirmish? (crucible/tadmor overlap what skirmish charges)

options.label: Straight to skirmish (Recommended)
options.description: Skip the redundant local crucible/tadmor runs — skirmish charges tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml anyway, plus the cloud build chain. fast already proved the enum gates. Fastest path to the acceptance signal.

options.label: crucible, then skirmish
options.description: Run the local crucible suite (tadmor/srjcl/pluml, no cloud) as a container smoke-test first, then skirmish. Catches container-only breakage before spending cloud build, at the cost of double-charging those crucibles.

options.label: tadmor + crucible, then skirmish
options.description: Full local container coverage (tadmor self-contained build + crucible) before skirmish. Most thorough locally, longest runtime, most redundancy with skirmish.

content.text:
`tadmor` suite green: **kludge-tadmor (1) + tadmor (59) = 60/60**, crucible charged and quenched cleanly. The full adversarial sortie set passed — containment holds, and the migrated `rbnne_enabled`/`rbnne_disabled` node-mode values fed the crucible config correctly.

Now the `crucible` suite (re-runs fast + charges tadmor/srjcl/pluml). One thing to watch: unlike the gauntlet, this suite has no kludge/ordain step, so srjcl/pluml charge against whatever hallmarks already exist locally — if those vessel images aren't present, those fixtures will fail on a missing image rather than a migration bug. Running and watching for that:

content.text:
Diagnosis complete — and it's a **precondition gap, not a migration bug**.

## Root cause

The `srjcl` nameplate's bottle vessel is `rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter`, which is `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=rbnve_conjure` with a **cloud-ordained GAR hallmark** (`RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=c260524152712-r260524152715`). Charging it requires that image present locally — but `docker images` shows no srjcl/jupyter image, and the `crucible` suite has **no kludge/ordain/summon step** (unlike skirmish/gauntlet, which run `onboarding-sequence` to build+ordain all four vessels first). So the charge dies on a missing bottle image during fixture setup, before any case runs.

`tadmor` charged fine in the very same run because its images were just kludge-built by `kludge-tadmor`. `srjcl` and `pluml` have no such build step in this suite.

## Why this is not a sprue regression

Three independent proofs the migration itself is sound:
1. **`fast` green** — the enum gates (`rbrv_conjure_baseline_valid`, `rbrn_invalid_entry/dns/access_mode`) accept the migrated `rbnve_*`/`rbnne_*` values.
2. **`tadmor` green (60/60)** — a full charge→adversarial-suite→quench cycle, with migrated `rbnne_enabled`/`rbnne_disabled` driving the crucible config map.
3. **`regime-validation` all_vessels passed** — every `rbrv.env`, including srjcl's `rbnve_conjure` anthropic-jupyter, parses and validates.

## Consequence for the re-run

The background restart will hit the **same failure at srjcl** — the cause is structural (no local image), not transient. It'll re-confirm fast + tadmor green, then die at srjcl again.

The `crucible` suite simply isn't self-sufficient on a clean station for the conjure/bind vessels. **`skirmish` is the right vehicle for srjcl/pluml/moriah crucibles** — it builds them via onboarding-sequence before charging. So this failure is expected-on-clean-station, and skirmish covers what crucible-standalone can't here.

How do you want to handle it?

content.text:
Triaged — and it's **environmental, not a migration bug**.

## What happened

```
rbfl_inscribe  Inscribe: QUEUED (poll 120/120)
rbfl_inscribe  ERROR: Inscribe: Build timeout after 120 polls
```

- `canonical-invest` **passed 3/3** — governor mantle + retriever/director invest all clean. The ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID flap did **not** hit this run.
- `onboarding-sequence`'s first case, `inscribe_reliquary`, submits a **Cloud Build** job to mirror tool images to GAR. The build sat `QUEUED` for all 120 polls and **never got a worker** — then timed out. stderr is empty; the build never failed on merit, it never started.

A build stuck QUEUED → never RUNNING is the **Cloud Build capacity/quota exhaustion** signature — the exact block you flagged for the gauntlet. The reliquary inscribe doesn't touch any sprue-migrated enum; this is the quota wall, not a missed migration site.

## The implication

`skirmish`'s onboarding-sequence needs Cloud Build (inscribe + cloud ordain), so **skirmish is quota-blocked today for the same reason as gauntlet** — as are `dogfight` and any conjure/ordain leg. With Cloud Build out, the only suites that can run to green are the fully-local ones.

## Where the macos regression actually stands

| Evidence | Status |
|----------|--------|
| Grep audit (all families + class-2 + rbtd- colophons) | ✅ Clean; caught + fixed the bubep_ harmonize miss |
| `fast` (enum gates on migrated values) | ✅ 112/112 |
| `tadmor` (full crucible, migrated node-modes) | ✅ 60/60 |
| `regime-validation` all_vessels (every rbrv.env incl. rbnve_conjure) | ✅ pass |
| `skirmish` / cloud-dependent legs | ⛔ Quota-blocked (not failing on merit) |

The migration is well-evidenced by the local suites + grep audit. The cloud-build legs land in the same out-of-band bucket you're already handling for the gauntlet.

Note: this skirmish run re-invested the SAs (canonical-invest) and left a Cloud Build stuck in QUEUED; no crucible was charged (it failed before that), so nothing local to quench.

How do you want to proceed?

message.content: If I rerun payor install, doesn't one of our suites mantle a governor and recreate the director/ retriever roles?

content.text:
Right on the mechanism — let me map the layers, because it changes whether a re-run helps.

The credential chain is three tiers:

1. **Payor (OAuth)** — `rbw-gPI.PayorInstall` ingests the OAuth payor credentials from a JSON key file. This is the *top* of the chain; it does **not** mantle a governor.
2. **Governor (SA)** — `rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor` creates/replaces the governor SA, using the payor.
3. **Director + Retriever (SAs)** — `rbw-adI` / `rbw-arI`, invested *by* the governor.

So your instinct is correct, but it's not payor-install that does the mantle — it's the **`canonical-invest` fixture**, which is exactly tiers 2+3:

```
canonical-invest:  rbtdrk_governor_mantle ✅
                   rbtdrk_retriever_invest ✅
                   rbtdrk_director_invest  ✅   (3/3 passed)
```

And here's the key point: **canonical-invest already ran and passed in this skirmish run.** The governor got re-mantled and both roles re-invested cleanly. Better still — the director SA it created then **successfully submitted** the reliquary Cloud Build: the build reached `QUEUED` and the status-poll kept getting valid responses for all 120 polls. So the credential chain is demonstrably healthy end-to-end.

The failure is **downstream of credentials**: the build was accepted into the queue but never got scheduled onto a worker (`QUEUED` → never `RUNNING` → timeout). That's Cloud Build **worker capacity/quota**, not an auth or account-state problem. Re-running payor install (or re-mantling) would refresh credentials that aren't broken — it wouldn't give the queued build a worker.

(This is also why I'm confident it's *not* the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID flap: that flap is a 401 on the build-status poll or vouch token-mint. Here the polls all succeeded — they just kept reporting QUEUED.)

So — what are you driving toward? A couple of possibilities, and I'll go where you point:

- **Confirming the mechanism** — then the answer is: credentials are fine, it's purely Cloud Build capacity, and re-install won't unblock today.
- **Suspecting the queue-stall is actually an auth/account-state symptom** — if you've seen QUEUED-stalls resolve before by re-establishing the payor/governor, I'm wrong to rule it out and we can re-run payor install → re-mantle → retry inscribe to test that theory.
- **Recovery/consistency concern** — making sure the SAs canonical-invest just re-invested are consistent with your local RBRA key files for later out-of-band cloud work.

Which is it?

content.text:
Yes — very plausible, and there are actually **two** distinct collisions from running both boxes concurrently against the *same* standing depot and the *same* shared SAs:

1. **Shared SA key-rotation race (the dominant one).** `canonical-invest` doesn't just mantle — it **divests and re-invests** the governor, retriever, and director SAs, minting fresh keys each time. Those are shared GCP identities. If macos and cerebro both run canonical-invest, each rotation invalidates the *other* box's freshly-minted local keys. Whichever ran last "wins"; the other box is left with stale keys — which is exactly cerebro's `Invalid JWT Signature` / `invalid_grant`. This is a genuine coordination hazard: canonical-invest is not safe to run on two stations sharing one SA set.

2. **Worker-pool contention (plausibly your QUEUED timeout).** Both onboarding-sequences submit reliquary-inscribe + ordain builds to the *same* depot's Cloud Build worker pool. A small/default private pool with limited concurrency will queue the second box's build behind the first — so the macos inscribe sat `QUEUED` for 120 polls waiting for a worker busy with cerebro's build (or the reverse). That matches "accepted into the queue, never scheduled, timed out" far better than a quota-exhaustion wall. So your earlier "quotaed out" may partly have been *self-inflicted contention*, not just account-level limits.

Net: the QUEUED timeout was likely the two runs timing each other out, and the credential staleness on cerebro is the rotation race. Worth not running canonical-invest on both boxes at once going forward (spook-worthy).

Now to the cerebro request — I'll run the **source-of-truth guard first** (step 1) before touching anything. These probes are read-only:

input.command:
./tt/rbw-acg.CheckGovernorCredential.sh; echo "EXIT_acg=$?"
./tt/rbw-acr.CheckRetrieverCredential.sh; echo "EXIT_acr=$?"
./tt/rbw-acd.CheckDirectorCredential.sh; echo "EXIT_acd=$?"
echo "=== local SA cred files (step 2) ==="
ls -l ../station-files/secrets/governor/rbra.env ../station-files/secrets/retriever/rbra.env ../station-files/secrets/director/rbra.env 2>&1
input.description: Run three SA credential probes and check local files

content.text:
Good call — the fixtures genuinely can't run concurrently against shared cloud state, and serializing is the right move for valid results today. Let me lay out the coordination model I'll design against, then give you the cerebro state-query prompt.

## The safety model (two tiers)

**Tier 1 — local-only, zero cross-box interference (run on either box, even simultaneously):**
- `fast` — pure validation, no GCP, no containers-that-matter.
- `tadmor` — local kludge-build + crucible, `--network` isolated, no GCP/depot.

These touch *nothing* shared. They're never a concurrency hazard.

**Tier 2 — shared cloud state, MUST serialize across both boxes (one box in flight at a time):**
- Anything with `canonical-invest` (skirmish, dogfight) — **rotates the shared SAs**; running it on one box invalidates the other's keys.
- Anything submitting **Cloud Build** (skirmish/dogfight onboarding-sequence, ordain) — shares the depot's worker pool → the QUEUED contention you hit.
- `summon` of GAR images, `service` suite probes — share GAR/SA but read-only-ish.

**The rotation rule:** after *any* box runs `canonical-invest`, that box holds the valid keys; the other box is stale until re-synced (the scp we just did). So the box that ran canonical-invest last is the source of truth.

To design the actual run order I need cerebro's current state. Here's a **read-only** prompt to paste into your cerebro Claude Code session:

```
Read-only state report — take NO mutating action. Do NOT run any test suite, do
NOT charge a crucible, do NOT run canonical-invest, do NOT submit any cloud build,
do NOT modify credentials. I'm coordinating a non-concurrent cross-box test
sequence with my macOS box and need your current state. Report these eight items
concisely:

1. Git: output of `git rev-parse HEAD`, `git status --short`, and `git branch --show-current`.
   State whether your tree is clean and what commit HEAD is at (I expect ece6bd214).

2. SA credential probes (these are READ-ONLY and safe — run them sequentially,
   directly, no pipes):
     tt/rbw-acg.CheckGovernorCredential.sh
     tt/rbw-acr.CheckRetrieverCredential.sh
     tt/rbw-acd.CheckDirectorCredential.sh
   Read ../logs-buk/last.txt after each; report PASS/FAIL per role. (My macOS box
   just scp'd you fresh governor/retriever/director keys, so all three should pass
   now — confirm.)

3. Depot identity: report the RBRD_* moniker and GCP project from your rbrd.env
   (the standing canonical depot you're pointed at), so I can confirm both boxes
   share the same depot.

4. Charged crucibles: `docker ps --format '{{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}'` — list any
   sentry/bottle/pentacle containers currently up, and note which nameplate they
   belong to (if any).

5. In-flight Cloud Build: is there any build YOU submitted still QUEUED or RUNNING
   right now? Check `gcloud builds list --ongoing` if available, plus your most
   recent ../logs-buk/ inscribe/ordain logs.

6. What fixtures/suites have you run today and their outcomes (pass/fail)?
   Summarize from ../logs-buk/ history (hist-* and same-* filenames + their tails).
   I specifically want to know what has already PASSED so we don't re-run it.

7. Local vessel images: `docker images` — which rbi_hm / vessel images are present
   locally (e.g. tadmor sentry+bottle, srjcl, pluml, moriah)?

8. Anything else about your session state I should know before I design the
   sequence (mid-run leftovers, partial charges, errors).

Report all eight; change nothing.
```

Once cerebro reports back, I'll design the exact interleaved sequence — which box runs what, in what order, with the SA-resync handoff points marked, so no Tier-2 step ever overlaps and every result is trustworthy. Paste cerebro's reply here when you have it.
